content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Andrew Morris 
Town Attorney 
10183 Truckee Airport Road 
Truckee, CA 96161-3306 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-284 
Dear Mr. Morris: 
January 28, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
. 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions 
such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Given that Truckee Town Councilmember David Polivy owns Tahoe Mountain Sports, a 
corporation and shop located across the street from the site of a planned Grocery Outlet, may he 
take part in governmental decisions involving the Grocery Outlet Project (or "the Project")? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Councilmember Polivy may not take part in governmental decisions involving the 
Project because it is reasonably foreseeable that governmental decisions pertaining to the Project 
will have material financial effects on Councilmember Polivy's interests related to Tahoe Mountain 
Sports. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-18-284 
Page No. 2 
Truckee Town Councilmember David Polivy owns a privately-held corporation called 
Tahoe Mountain Sports, Inc., which operates a shop called Tahoe Mountain Sports. Tahoe 
Mountain Sports is located at 11200 Donner Pass Road, Unit 53, in Truckee. Tahoe Mountain 
Sports sells a variety of clothing and equipment suitable for recreational pursuits available around 
Truckee, and the "mountain lifestyle" in general. The shop is popular with both Truckee residents 
and the large numbers of visitors who come to Truckee. Tahoe Mountain Sports is located in a 
leased space in a shopping center that contains a Safeway grocery store and Rite-Aid drug store, 
along with various smaller stores. The lease has a remaining value in excess of $2,000. 
Before Councilmember Polivy was elected, the Town Council had approved a grocery store 
project to be located at approximately 11213 Donner Pass Road, across the street from Tahoe 
Mountain Sports, approximately 315 feet away. The Project site is currently undeveloped. The 
Project is expected to draw some business away from the two existing grocery stores in Truckee, 
but is also expected to capture grocery sales that are currently occurring in nearby communities or 
in Reno, Nevada, the largest nearby metropolitan area. A traffic consultant has estimated that the 
Grocery Outlet Project will generate 1,525 vehicle trips per day, bringing at least that many people 
to the vicinity of Tahoe Mountain Sports. The Project is also expected to slightly worsen traffic 
congestion along Donner Pass Road, which is the primary access route for Tahoe Mountain Sports' 
business. 
Truckee is currently facing a lawsuit involving the Grocery Outlet Project, which has not yet 
begun construction. The suit seeks to invalidate the Town's approval of the Grocery Outlet Project, 
and the Town Council will have to make decisions concerning the litigation, including whether to 
defend the suit. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or in any 
way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows 
or has reason to know he has a financial interest. 
Interests 
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on, 
among other things: 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
$2,000 or more or is a director, officer, partner, trustee employee, or manager. 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 
or more. 
• 
Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 


File No. A-18-284 
Page No. 3 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(Section 87103(a)-(c).) 
Based on the facts you provided, Councilmember Polivy has several interests that may be 
implicated by governmental decisions directly pertaining to the Grocery Outlet Project. These 
interests include interests in Tahoe Mountain Sports as a business entity and source of income as 
well as his interest in the leased property. Accordingly, we must determine whether the 
governmental decisions pertaining to the Project will have a reasonably foreseeable, material 
financial effect on those interests. 
Reasonable Foreseeability 
The standard for foreseeability varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Councilmember Polivy's business, lease and source of income interests are 
not explicitly involved in the decisions at issue, in that none of these interests are the subject of, or 
named as a party in, this decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) 
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial 
effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) If the 
financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public 
official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
Materiality 
A financial effect on a business entity is material if a prudent person with sufficient 
information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would 
contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a 
privately-held business entity. (Regulation 18702.1 (b ). ) Examples of decisions that may be 
applicable include those that: 
• 
Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business entity is 
engaged· 
• 
Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies; 
• 
Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood, such as redevelopment projects, 
traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or 
permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives; or 
• 
Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that 
would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the 
project. 
(Regulation 18702.l(b)(2)-(5).) 


File No. A-18-284 
Page No. 4 
The same standards apply in assessing the materiality of a financial effect on a source of 
income that is a business entity. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) 
In relevant part, the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a governmental decision on 
any real property in which a governmental official has a leasehold interest is material whenever the 
governmental decision will increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property. 2 
You have noted that the Project site is located only 315 feet away from Tahoe Mountain 
Sports and, once completed, the Grocery Outlet is expected to generate at least 1,525 vehicle trips 
per day, bringing at least that many people to the vicinity of Councilmember Polivy's store. You 
further specified that the project is also expected to slightly worsen traffic congestion along Donner 
Pass Road, the primary access route to Tahoe Mou~tain Sports. 
While it does not appear that Grocery Outlet and Tahoe Mountain Sports will compete for 
customers, the Project will draw significantly more people to the area, which would likely affect the 
amount of business that Tahoe Mountain Sports receives, and therefore it is reasonably foreseeable 
that the decisions, such as whether to defend a lawsuit and proceed with the Project, would 
contribute to a change in the value of Tahoe Mountain Sports. For that same reason, it is 
reasonably foreseeable that such decisions would result in a change to the value of Councilmember 
Polivy's leasehold interest in the real property where his business is currently located. 
Because it is reasonably foreseeable that governmental decisions pertaining to the Project 
will have material financial effects on his interests, Councilmember Polivy has disqualifying 
financial interests. Therefore, he is prohibited from making, participating in making, or in any way 
attempting to use his official position to influence those governmental decisions, and must recuse 
himself from those decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division 
2 Citation provided is to Regulation 18702.2 as amended by the Commission on January 17, 2019.

will have material financial effects on Councilmember Polivy's interests related to Tahoe Mountain 
Sports.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Given that Truckee Town Councilmember David Polivy owns Tahoe Mountain Sports, a 
corporation and shop located across the street from the site of a planned Grocery Outlet, may he 
take part in governmental decisions involving the Grocery Outlet Project (or "the Project")?

CONCLUSION: No. Councilmember Polivy may not take part in governmental decisions involving the 
Project because it is reasonably foreseeable that governmental decisions pertaining to the Project 
will have material financial effects on Councilmember Polivy's interests related to Tahoe Mountain 
Sports.
